title: Missed delivery window with save offer
trigger_phrases: I took off work for this
customer_profile_signals: high churn risk after failed stop
answer: I'm sorry we missed that stop. Let me lock in the soonest replacement window for you, and if the missed delivery was on our side I'll note the account for the standard service-recovery review so you know exactly what happens next.
retrieval_text:
Missed delivery window with save offer
Customer intents: report.missed_delivery, ask.delivery_status, express.frustration, consider.canceling
Trigger phrases: nobody showed up | you missed my delivery | I took off work for this | this is the second time | I might switch companies
Keywords: missed delivery, late driver, service failure, delivery credit, cancel service
Customer profile signals: keep-full customer, recent service complaint, high churn risk after failed stop
Answer: I'm sorry we missed that stop. Let me lock in the soonest replacement window for you, and if the missed delivery was on our side I'll note the account for the standard service-recovery review so you know exactly what happens next.
Rep guidance: Lead with a direct apology before explaining anything. Confirm the replacement window first, then mention a possible service credit only after you verify the miss was company-caused and the account qualifies under policy.
Next best action: Book the earliest available delivery window and review whether the account qualifies for a service recovery credit.
Authorization notes: Only promise a service credit when the missed delivery is confirmed as company-caused and the account qualifies under local policy.

next_best_action: Book the earliest available delivery window and review whether the account qualifies for a service recovery credit.
do_not_say: The route was busy
There's nothing I can do
You'll just have to wait
authorization_notes: Only promise a service credit when the missed delivery is confirmed as company-caused and the account qualifies under local policy.
source_title: Synthetic Mock Propane Agent Assist Playbook
citation_label: Synthetic Mock Propane Agent Assist Playbook > service-recovery > Missed delivery window with save offer

title: Out of propane with heat risk
answer: If the tank is empty and you may lose heat, I'm treating this as an urgent delivery issue right now. I'll confirm your address and involve dispatch, and if you smell gas or hear hissing, leave the area and call 911 instead of staying near the system. Once a tank has fully run out, please do not try to relight appliances yourself because a qualified leak-check and restart are required before service is safely restored.
retrieval_text:
Out of propane with heat risk
Customer intents: report.out_of_propane, request.emergency_delivery, report.no_heat
Trigger phrases: we have no heat | the tank is empty | we ran out last night | my kids are freezing | I need propane today
Keywords: empty tank, out of gas, emergency delivery, no heat, same day
Customer profile signals: heat-dependent household, elderly or children in home, winter weather urgency
Answer: If the tank is empty and you may lose heat, I'm treating this as an urgent delivery issue right now. I'll confirm your address and involve dispatch, and if you smell gas or hear hissing, leave the area and call 911 instead of staying near the system. Once a tank has fully run out, please do not try to relight appliances yourself because a qualified leak-check and restart are required before service is safely restored.
Rep guidance: Do not make the customer repeat the emergency more than once. Verify whether there is no heat, ask immediately whether there is any gas smell or hissing, route leak symptoms to the leak-response path, and keep the focus on dispatch plus qualified restart requirements rather than billing debate during the first response.
Next best action: Escalate to emergency dispatch, screen for leak symptoms, and set the expectation that a qualified leak-check and restart are required after a full runout.
Authorization notes: Follow local emergency-dispatch, leak-check, and restart policy; do not imply appliances can be relit before a qualified technician clears the system.
rep_guidance: Do not make the customer repeat the emergency more than once. Verify whether there is no heat, ask immediately whether there is any gas smell or hissing, route leak symptoms to the leak-response path, and keep the focus on dispatch plus qualified restart requirements rather than billing debate during the first response.
next_best_action: Escalate to emergency dispatch, screen for leak symptoms, and set the expectation that a qualified leak-check and restart are required after a full runout.
do_not_say: We'll get there when we can
Call back later
Let me explain your balance first
escalation_reason: Customer reports an empty tank with potential loss of heat; a full runout requires urgent delivery and qualified restart handling.
authorization_notes: Follow local emergency-dispatch, leak-check, and restart policy; do not imply appliances can be relit before a qualified technician clears the system.
source_title: Synthetic Mock Propane Agent Assist Playbook
citation_label: Synthetic Mock Propane Agent Assist Playbook > service-recovery > Out of propane with heat risk

title: Low tank but not empty yet
trigger_phrases: I don't want to run out
answer: Since you're low but not empty, let's work on preventing a runout. I'll note your current gauge reading, check the fastest delivery window, and make sure you know whether automatic delivery would help you avoid this kind of close call going forward.
retrieval_text:
Low tank but not empty yet
Customer intents: ask.delivery_status, report.low_tank, seek.reassurance
Trigger phrases: I'm down to 15 percent | the gauge is really low | how soon can someone come | I don't want to run out
Keywords: low gauge, 15 percent, 20 percent, delivery timing, run out
Customer profile signals: will-call customer, high recent usage, cold-weather burn rate
Answer: Since you're low but not empty, let's work on preventing a runout. I'll note your current gauge reading, check the fastest delivery window, and make sure you know whether automatic delivery would help you avoid this kind of close call going forward.
Rep guidance: If the customer is below the usual refill threshold, move quickly to scheduling rather than giving a lecture about usage. If this is a repeat low-tank call from a will-call customer, suggest switching to automatic delivery once the immediate concern is handled.
Next best action: Schedule the earliest practical delivery and recommend auto-delivery if the customer repeatedly calls at low tank levels.
Authorization notes: Use the standard refill threshold and route-priority rules for the operating region.
rep_guidance: If the customer is below the usual refill threshold, move quickly to scheduling rather than giving a lecture about usage. If this is a repeat low-tank call from a will-call customer, suggest switching to automatic delivery once the immediate concern is handled.

do_not_say: You should have called sooner
15 percent is fine, don't worry about it

title: Explaining auto-delivery versus will-call
trigger_phrases: do I have to call every time
I don't want to keep watching the gauge
answer: Auto-delivery means we watch expected usage and schedule fills before the tank gets critically low, while will-call means you monitor the gauge and contact us when you want a delivery. If you don't want to keep watching the tank, auto-delivery is usually the easier option.
retrieval_text:
Explaining auto-delivery versus will-call
Customer intents: ask.plan_difference, consider.auto_delivery, manage.account
Trigger phrases: what's the difference | do I have to call every time | how does keep-full work | I don't want to keep watching the gauge
Keywords: auto delivery, keep full, will call, monitoring usage, delivery plan
Customer profile signals: busy household, seasonal usage swings, prior low-tank anxiety
Answer: Auto-delivery means we watch expected usage and schedule fills before the tank gets critically low, while will-call means you monitor the gauge and contact us when you want a delivery. If you don't want to keep watching the tank, auto-delivery is usually the easier option.
Rep guidance: Position auto-delivery as a convenience and runout-prevention tool, not a hard sell. If the customer prefers control, explain the responsibility that comes with will-call so expectations stay clear.
Next best action: Recommend auto-delivery for customers who dislike monitoring the gauge or have already had low-tank scares.
Authorization notes: Describe the plan options accurately and confirm any enrollment change before submitting it.
rep_guidance: Position auto-delivery as a convenience and runout-prevention tool, not a hard sell. If the customer prefers control, explain the responsibility that comes with will-call so expectations stay clear.
next_best_action: Recommend auto-delivery for customers who dislike monitoring the gauge or have already had low-tank scares.
do_not_say: Auto-delivery is always better for everyone
Will-call customers are harder to serve
authorization_notes: Describe the plan options accurately and confirm any enrollment change before submitting it.
source_title: Synthetic Mock Propane Agent Assist Playbook
citation_label: Synthetic Mock Propane Agent Assist Playbook > account > Explaining auto-delivery versus will-call

title: Budget billing for seasonal bill spikes
trigger_phrases: why is this month so much higher
answer: Budget billing can smooth your propane costs into steadier monthly payments so winter spikes are easier to manage. It is meant to make the payments more predictable, although the amount can still be reviewed if your actual usage changes a lot.
retrieval_text:
Budget billing for seasonal bill spikes
Customer intents: ask.bill_explanation, seek.lower_monthly_cost, complain.high_winter_bill
Trigger phrases: my winter bill was huge | can I spread this out | I need steadier payments | why is this month so much higher
Keywords: budget billing, level pay, even payments, winter usage, monthly average
Customer profile signals: seasonal usage shock, fixed-income concern, first winter with propane
Answer: Budget billing can smooth your propane costs into steadier monthly payments so winter spikes are easier to manage. It is meant to make the payments more predictable, although the amount can still be reviewed if your actual usage changes a lot.
Rep guidance: Acknowledge the bill shock before teaching the plan. Keep the explanation simple: steadier monthly payments now, periodic review later, and less surprise during the coldest months.
Next best action: Offer a budget billing enrollment quote and explain when the plan amount is reviewed.
Authorization notes: Explain that budget billing changes payment timing, not underlying usage cost, and quote only the approved plan amount.
rep_guidance: Acknowledge the bill shock before teaching the plan. Keep the explanation simple: steadier monthly payments now, periodic review later, and less surprise during the coldest months.
next_best_action: Offer a budget billing enrollment quote and explain when the plan amount is reviewed.
do_not_say: It will make the bill cheaper
You won't ever have an adjustment
authorization_notes: Explain that budget billing changes payment timing, not underlying usage cost, and quote only the approved plan amount.
source_title: Synthetic Mock Propane Agent Assist Playbook
citation_label: Synthetic Mock Propane Agent Assist Playbook > billing > Budget billing for seasonal bill spikes

Payment plan for temporary hardship

Answer: If paying the full balance today is the problem, let's look at the payment arrangement options we can offer instead of leaving it open-ended. I can walk you through the timing and amounts and also check whether budget billing would make future months easier to manage.

title: Gas smell, leak, or hissing sound
answer: If you smell propane or hear hissing, please leave the area right away without using switches, flames, or appliances, and call 911 from a safe location. Once you're safe, we'll follow the emergency response steps on our side.
retrieval_text:
Gas smell, leak, or hissing sound
Customer intents: report.gas_smell, report.leak, seek.safety_help
Trigger phrases: I smell gas | it smells like rotten eggs | I hear hissing | is this dangerous | hay olor a gas
Keywords: gas smell, rotten eggs, propane leak, hissing, evacuate, 911
Customer profile signals: life safety event, possible occupied home, active hazard on site
Answer: If you smell propane or hear hissing, please leave the area right away without using switches, flames, or appliances, and call 911 from a safe location. Once you're safe, we'll follow the emergency response steps on our side.
Rep guidance: Use short, direct instructions and stay in command of the call. Do not let the conversation drift into billing, scheduling preferences, or appliance troubleshooting.
Next best action: Instruct evacuation and 911, then alert the internal emergency response path and supervisor.
Authorization notes: Safety instructions override normal billing or scheduling talk; reps must not troubleshoot or suggest relighting.
rep_guidance: Use short, direct instructions and stay in command of the call. Do not let the conversation drift into billing, scheduling preferences, or appliance troubleshooting.
next_best_action: Instruct evacuation and 911, then alert the internal emergency response path and supervisor.
do_not_say: It's probably nothing
Try relighting the appliance
Can you go check the tank for me
escalation_reason: Possible propane leak or active safety hazard.
authorization_notes: Safety instructions override normal billing or scheduling talk; reps must not troubleshoot or suggest relighting.
